sentences.text: Urinary Incontinence Number: 0223 Applicable CPT / HCPCS / ICD-10 Codes Scope of Policy This Clinical Policy Bulletin addresses urinary incontinence.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: I. Medical Necessity Aetna considers multi-channel urodynamic studies medically necessary when the member has both symptoms and physical findings of urinary incontinence/voiding dysfunctions (such as stress incontinence, overactive bladder, lower urinary tract symptoms) and there is consideration by the provider to perform invasive, potentially morbid or irreversible treatments after conservative management has been tried and failed.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers the following urinary incontinence interventions medically necessary when criteria are met: A. Artificial Urinary Sphincter Aetna considers the implantation of an artificial urinary sphincter (AUS) medically necessary for the treatment of urinary incontinence (UI) due to intrinsic urethral sphincter deficiency (IUSD) for members with any of the following indications: 1.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Children with intractable UI due to IUSD who are refractory to behavioral or pharmacological therapies and are unsuitable candidates for other types of surgical procedures for correction of UI; or 2.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Members who are 6 or more months post-prostatectomy who have had no improvement in the severity of UI despite trials of behavioral and/or pharmacological therapies; or 3.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Members with epispadias-exstrophy in whom bladder neck reconstruction has failed; or 4.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Women with intractable UI who have failed behavioral or pharmacological, and other surgical treatments.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Aetna considers the artificial urinary sphincter experimental, investigational, or unproven for all other indications because its effectiveness for indications other than the ones listed above has not been established.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: B. Peri-Urethral Injections of Bulking Agents Aetna considers peri-urethral injections of bulking agents that are cleared by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for UI (e.g., Bulkamid (polyacrylamide hydrogel), Coaptite [calcium hydroxylapatite], Contigen [glutaraldehyde crossed-linked collagen], Durasphere [carbon-coated spheres/beads], Macroplastique [polydimethylsiloxane], Uryx [ethylene vinyl alcohol copolymer]) medically necessary for the management of members with UI resulting from intrinsic sphincter deficiency that is refractory to conservative management (e.g., Kegel exercises, biofeedback, electrical stimulation, and/or pharmacotherapies).
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Members whose incontinence does not improve after 3 treatments with bulking agents are considered treatment failures and are not likely to respond to this therapy.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In such cases, further treatment with bulking agents is not considered medically necessary.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers injection of peri-urethral bulking agents for UI experimental, investigational, or unproven for neurogenic bladder and all other indications.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: C. Implantable Sacral Nerve Stimulators (e.g., Axonics and InterStim) Aetna considers permanent implantation (Stage 2) of FDA-approved implantable sacral nerve stimulators (e.g., Axonics and InterStim) medically necessary for the treatment of urge UI or symptoms of urge-frequency when all of the following criteria are met: 1.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: The member has experienced urge UI or symptoms of urge-frequency for at least 6 months and the condition has resulted in significant disability (the frequency and/or severity of symptoms are limiting the member's ability to participate in daily activities); and 2.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Pharmacotherapies (i.e., at least 2 different anti-cholinergic drugs or an anti-cholinergic and a beta-3 adrenergic receptor agonist (mirabegron)) as well as behavioral treatments (e.g., pelvic floor exercise, biofeedback, timed voids, and fluid management) have failed; and 3.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Test stimulation (Stage 1) provides at least 50 % decrease in symptoms.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: A test stimulation (Stage 1) of the device is considered medically necessary for members who meet selection criteria a and b above.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna also considers permanent implantation (Stage 2) of a sacral nerve stimulator medically necessary for the treatment of non-obstructive urinary retention when all of the following criteria are met: 1.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: The member has experienced urinary retention for at least 6 months and the condition has resulted in significant disability (the frequency and/or severity of symptoms are limiting the member's ability to participate in daily activities); and 2.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Pharmacotherapies (e.g., alpha blockers and antibiotics for urinary tract infections) as well as intermittent catheterization have failed or are not well-tolerated; and 3.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: A test stimulation (Stage 1) of the device has provided at least 50 % decrease in residual urine volume.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Aetna considers test stimulation (Stage 1) of the right and left sides (where leads are placed bilaterally; and each side is tested sequentially during a single visit) medically necessary for members who meet selection criteria a and b above for treatment of urgent incontinence and non-obstructive urinary retention.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: No more than 6 total test stimulations (Stage 1) are considered medically necessary.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers bilateral implantation of a permanent sacral nerve stimulator experimental, investigational, or unproven for the treatment of UI and non- obstructive urinary retention because the effectiveness of bilateral implantation of a permanent sacral nerve stimulator has not been established.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers removal of an implantable sacral nerve stimulator medically necessary even where the initial implantation of the implantable sacral nerve stimulator was not indicated.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers the use of implantable sacral nerve stimulator experimental, investigational, or unproven for all other indications because its effectiveness for indications other than the ones listed above has not been established.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: According to the product labeling, implantable sacral nerve stimulator is contraindicated and has no proven value for individuals who have not demonstrated an appropriate response to test stimulation (Stage 1) or are unable to operate the neurostimulator.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Exclusions Implantable sacral nerve stimulator has no proven value for individuals with mechanical obstruction such as benign prostatic hypertrophy, or urethral stricture; persons with stress incontinence; and individuals with neurologic disease origins, such as multiple sclerosis or diabetes with peripheral nerve involvement.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Implantable sacral nerve stimulator has not been shown to be effective for urinary retention, urinary frequency-urgency syndrome or urge urinary incontinence due to these causes.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: D. Vaginal Cones Aetna considers weighted vaginal cones (vaginal weights) medically necessary DME when they are used in combination with a structured pelvic floor muscle exercise (Kegel's exercise) program for the treatment of simple (pure) stress UI.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers vaginal cones experimental, investigational, or unproven for other indications because their effectiveness for indications other than the ones listed above has not been established.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: E. Pessary (Bladder Neck Support Prosthesis) Aetna considers a pessary, a plastic device that fits into the vagina to help support the uterus and bladder, medically necessary DME for the treatment of women with stress or mixed UI, and for the treatment of pelvic organ (uterine) prolapse.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers a pessary experimental, investigational, or unproven for other indications because its effectiveness for indications other than the ones listed above has not been established.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: F. Tension-Free Vaginal Tape Procedure Aetna considers the tension-free vaginal tape (TVT) procedure medically necessary for the treatment of stress UI when women with intractable UI have failed behavioral and/or pharmacological treatments.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers the TVT procedure experimental, investigational, or unproven for other indications (except for the treatment of pelvic organ prolapse complicated by stress UI - see CPB 0858 - Organ Prolapse: Selected Procedures) because its effectiveness for indications other than the one listed above has not been established.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: G. Transobturator Tape Procedure Aetna considers the transobturator tape (TOT) procedure medically necessary for the treatment of stress UI when women with intractable stress UI have failed behavioral and/or pharmacological treatments.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers the TOT procedure experimental, investigational, or unproven for urge urinary incontinence and other indications because its effectiveness for indications other than the one listed above has not been established.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: H. Colposuspension and Sling Procedures Aetna considers colposuspension and conventional sub-urethral sling procedures (e.g., the Solyx single-incision sling) medically necessary for persons with stress UI that is refractory to conservative management (e.g., pelvic floor muscle training, electrical stimulation, and biofeedback).
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers the colposuspension and sub-urethral sling procedures experimental, investigational, or unproven for other indications because their effectiveness for indications other than the one listed above has not been established.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: I. Biofeedback For biofeedback for UI, see CPB 0132 - Biofeedback.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: J. Percutaneous Tibial Nerve Stimulation Aetna considers percutaneous tibial nerve stimulation (PTNS) (e.g., the NURO Percutaneous Tibial Neuromodulation System (Medtronic, Minneapolis MN), and the Urgent PC Neuromodulation System (Uroplasty, Inc., Minneapolis, MN)) medically necessary for the treatment of members with idiopathic overactive bladder (urge UI or urge-frequency) when they meet the first 2 criteria listed for Implantable Sacral Nerve Stimulators (e.g., Axonics and InterStim) (policy section I.B.3a and I.B.3b for the treatment of urge urinary incontinence or symptoms of urge-frequency).
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: In general, 12 treatments (once-weekly) with PTNS are needed for symptom relief.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: If the member fails to improve after 12 PTNS treatments, continued treatment is considered not medically necessary.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: If the member improves after 12 PTNS treatments, continued monthly treatments are considered medically necessary as long as the member’s symptoms remain improved.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers percutaneous tibial nerve stimulation experimental, investigational, or unproven when criteria are not met.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: K. Transurethral Radiofrequency Therapy (Renessa Procedure) Aetna considers transurethral radiofrequency therapy (Renessa procedure) medically necessary for the treatment of stress UI in non-pregnant women who are either not able or not willing to undergo surgery for their condition.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: L. Urethral Inserts Aetna considers urethral inserts medically necessary for the treatment of female stress UI.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Aetna considers urethral inserts experimental, investigational, or unproven for other indications because their effectiveness for indications other than the one listed above has not been established.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: M. Cunningham Clamp Aetna considers the Cunningham clamp medically necessary for the treatment of post- prostatectomy urinary incontinence in men with stress incontinence and good bladder storage function.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: N. Intravaginal Electrical Stimulation Aetna considers intravaginal electrical stimulation of the pelvic floor medically necessary for women with stress, urgency or mixed urinary incontinence. II.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Policy Limitations and Exclusions Pelvic Muscle Trainers Note: Aetna does not cover the Athena pelvic muscle trainer, Gyneflex, Kegelmaster, or similar devices for the treatment of UI because these devices are considered exercise machines, and they do not meet Aetna's definition of covered durable medical equipment (DME).
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Most Aetna plans exclude coverage of exercise devices.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Please check benefit plan descriptions for details.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: In addition, such exercise devices do not meet Aetna's definition of covered DME because they are not primarily medical in nature and/or are normally of use to persons who do not have an illness or injury. IV.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: needed for one month E0736 Transcutaneous tibial nerve stimulator [ZIDA Wearable Neuromodulation System] Transcutaneous tibial nerve stimulator, controlled by phone application [Vivally E0737 System] Code Code Description ICD-10 codes not covered for indications listed in the CPB: N32.81 Overactive bladder N39.41 Urge incontinence R35.0 Frequency of micturition R39.15 Urgency of urination Urinary incontinence (UI) is the inability to voluntarily control voiding of urine from the bladder.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: It affects people of all ages especially elderly women.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Urinary incontinence is not part of the normal aging process; however, age-related changes in the functioning of the lower urinary tract make the elderly more susceptible to this malady.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: There are 4 prevalent types of UI in adults: I. stress incontinence, II. urge incontinence, III. overflow incontinence, and IV. mixed stress and urge incontinence. I. stress incontinence, II. urge incontinence, III. overflow incontinence, and IV. mixed stress and urge incontinence.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Stress incontinence is more common but less difficult to control than urge incontinence.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Mixed incontinence is more prevalent than urge incontinence in women while the latter is more commonly seen in men.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In women, stress incontinence (SI) is generally caused by an incompetent urethral mechanism which arises from damage to the urethral sphincter or weakening of the bladder neck support that typically occurred during childbirth.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Some women develop SI as a consequence of multiple anti-incontinence procedures resulting in a condition known as intrinsic urethral sphincter deficiency.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In men, SI is usually a consequence of operations for benign prostatic hypertrophy or prostatic carcinoma.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The mechanisms of post- prostatectomy UI may involve bladder dysfunction, sphincter incompetence, and mixed.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Urge incontinence occurs when one senses the urge to void, but is unable to prevent leakage of urine before reaching the bathroom.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: It is usually associated with an overactivity of the detrusor muscle.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Overflow incontinence is the result of the bladder's inability to empty normally.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Implantable Sacral Nerve Stimulators for the Treatment of Neurogenic Bladder In a meta-analysis, Sun and Song (2025) examined the safety and effectiveness of SNM in the treatment of neurogenic bladder (NB) and neurogenic bowel dysfunction (NBD).
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: These investigators carried out a systematic literature search using PubMed and Web of Science up to August 2024, focusing on studies related to SNM treatment for NB or NBD.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: After assessing the quality of the studies, data were extracted and analyzed using Review Manager 5.3.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: A total of 15 studies involving 573 patients were included.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: After SNM treatment, the patients showed significant improvements in key outcome measures, including voiding frequency per 24 hours (V24) (weighted mean difference [WMD] -4.08; 95 % CI: -6.80 to -1.35; p = 0.003), mean urine volume per micturition (MUV) (WMD 123.60; 95 % CI: 93.17 to 154.03; p < 0.001), number of leakage episodes per 24 hours (L24) (WMD -4.27; 95 % CI: -5.79 to -2.74; p < 0.001), number of nocturia (WMD -2.48; 95 % CI: -2.62 to -2.35; p < 0.001), clean intermittent self-catheterization per 24 hours (WMD -2.35; 95 % CI: -2.98 to -1.71; p < 0.001), bladder compliance (WMD 9.09; 95 % CI: 2.31 to 15.87; p = 0.009), maximum detrusor pressure (MDP) during storage phase (WMD -14.76; 95 % CI: -18.63 to -10.88; p < 0.001), maximum urine flow rate (Qmax) (WMD 6.50; 95 % CI: 4.21 to 8.80; p < 0.001), MCC (WMD 66.28; 95 % CI: 2.83 to 129.73; p = 0.04), Wexner score (WMD -9.98; 95 % CI: -13.65 to -6.31; p < 0.001), and NBD score (WMD -6.31; 95 % CI: -6.89 to -5.73; p < 0.001).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors concluded that these findings indicated that SNM was safe and effective in treating NB or NBD.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: In a meta-analysis, Yu et al (2025) examined available evidence of non-invasive or minimally invasive neuromodulation therapies in improving urodynamic outcomes, voiding diaries, and quality of life (QOL) in patients with neurogenic lower urinary tract dysfunction (NLUTD) after spinal cord injury (SCI).
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: These researchers carried out a comprehensive search of 10 data-bases from inception until August 30, 2023.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) examining the effects of conventional treatment (CT) and CT combined with sham stimulation (SS), transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), sacral nerve magnetic stimulation (SNMS), TMS+SNMS, sacral pulsed electromagnetic field therapy (SPEMFT), sacral transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (STENS), sacral dermatomal transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (SDTENS), bladder & sacral transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (B&STENS), transcutaneous tibial nerve stimulation (TTNS), transcutaneous electrical acupoint stimulation (TEAS), pelvic floor electrical stimulation (PFES), or pelvic floor biofeedback therapy (PFBFBT) on PVR volume, MCC, V24, MUV, Qmax, MDP, maximum voiding volume (MVV), L24, lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) score, and spinal cord injury-QOL (SCI-QOL)score in patients with NLUTD after SCI were included.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Two researchers independently extracted data on study characteristics and outcomes following the PRISMA statement.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: The Cochrane risk of bias tool (2.0) was used to assess the quality of RCTs.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: A total of 52 RCTs with 2,884 participants were included.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CT+TMS was able to remarkably decrease PVR (mean difference [MD], -132.14; 95 % CI: -230.97 to -33.31) and increase MUV (MD, 147.79; 95 % CI: 64.51 to 231.06).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: CT+SNMS ranked high in improving V24 (MD, 2.76; 95 % CI: 1.26 to 4.25) and reducing L24 (MD, -2.73; 95 % CI: -4.46 to -1.01); CT+TMS+SNMS maximized the reduction of SCI-QOL scores (MD, -1.52; 95 % CI: -2.97 to -0.25) and ranked 2nd in both reducing PVR and improving MCC; CT+SPEMFT had a significant advantage in improving MCC (MD, 83.31; 95 % CI: 39.73 to 126.88) and increasing Qmax (MD, 5.91; 95 % CI: 2.99 to 8.84); improvement in MDP was highly ranked by CT+TTNS (MD, 9.46; 95 % CI: 2.15 to 16.76).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors concluded that CT combined with magnetic stimulation therapy provided more benefits than its combination with electrical stimulation.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: These researchers stated that TMS+SNMS appeared to be a promising non-invasive neuromodulation technique in managing NLUTD after SCI.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Moreover, these researchers stated that high-quality RCTs are needed to validate these findings.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Buford et al (2024) stated that NLUTD is highly prevalent among patients with neurologic disorders.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Some studies have reported that implantable neuromodulation could improve symptoms of NLUTD.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In a retrospective, single-center study, these investigators described their experience with sacral and pudendal neuromodulation in patients with NLUTD.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: They carried out a retrospective chart review of patients with "neurogenic bladder" ICD-9/10 (International Classification of Diseases, 9th Revision/10th Revision) code was carried out at a single institution.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: This included patients from 2008 to 2020 who underwent stage 1 neuromodulation trial.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Demographic and clinical information was collected, including neurologic diagnosis, the character of patients' voiding symptoms, the presence or absence of fecal incontinence, the need for intermittent catheterization, and whether patients had sufficient (greater than 50 %) improvement in their symptoms to undergo stage 2 IPG placement.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: These researchers identified 82 patients with neurologic diagnoses who underwent stage 1 neuromodulation.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: The most common diagnoses were diabetic cystopathy (17.07 %), spinal surgery (17.07 %), and SCI (12.20 %).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The most commonly reported symptoms were urinary urgency, and urge urinary incontinence.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Overall, 59 patients (71.95 %) advanced to stage 2 IPG placement including 72 % of patients with sacral leads and 76 % with pudendal leads.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors concluded that neuromodulation was feasible and effective in the treatment of NLUTD.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Moreover, these researchers stated that further investigations into its utilization are needed.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: The authors stated that drawbacks of this trial included its retrospective design; thus, they could not examine potential causality, delve deeply into patient history, and standardize data collection.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The small sample size (n = 82 subjects; 10 patients with SCI) limited the generalizability of the findings.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: However, the sample size was on par with similar studies that examined neuromodulation for the management of NLUTD.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Furthermore, the study was carried out at a single-single trial, which may have restricted the diversity of the patient population.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: These investigators also stated that it was important to acknowledge that due to the focus of this study on the short trial phase, long-term effectiveness and AEs were not specifically recorded or analyzed.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In a subsequent meta-analysis that included SNM for non-neurogenic voiding symptoms, the incidence of AEs was comparable to those reported in the van Ophoven meta-analysis; thus, it was reasonable to assume potential long-term AEs would be consistent with other studies.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: This highlighted the need for future research with prospective designs, larger sample sizes, multi-center collaborations, validated questionnaires, and longer follow-up periods to provide more robust and comprehensive evidence.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Furthermore, an UpToDate review on “Chronic complications of spinal cord injury and disease” (Abrams and Wakasa, 2024) states that “Studies of implanted sacral nerve modulators show promise as a treatment for urinary iոϲοոtiոеոcе following SСΙ.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: For patients with unsatisfactory response to medical and catheter management, other treatments, bladder augmentation, urinary diversion, sphincterotomy, urethral stent, and electrical implantation devices can be considered in selected cases, although there is limited evidence of their comparative efficacy”.

sentences.text: Micro-Ablative Radiofrequency for the Treatment of Over-Active Bladder Slongo et al (2025) stated that energy therapies have been suggested as potential treatments for OAB; however, there are few studies examining their effectiveness.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In a pilot study, these researchers compared the effects of fractional micro-ablative RF to sham treatment.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: This trial was carried out with 77 women diagnosed with OAB, randomized into 2 groups: one receiving 3 monthly sessions of fractional micro- ablative RF and the other receiving sham treatment, both combined with behavioral therapy.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Assessments were carried out at baseline and 30 days after therapy using validated questionnaires for urinary symptoms (ICIQ-Short Form, ICIQ-OAB, and ICIQ-QOL), as well as vaginal and sexual function, and pelvic floor muscle functionality.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Both treatment groups showed significant improvements in all validated questionnaires evaluating urinary and vaginal symptoms (p < 0.001), with no significant differences between them.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The ICIQ-OAB scores improved significantly in both the RF group (-4.5 points; p < 0.001) and the sham group (-4.5 points; p < 0.001), with no significant differences between the groups (p = 0.812).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Furthermore, there were no improvements noted in sexual function or vaginal trophism in the RF group.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: However, assessments of endurance, resistance, and fast contractions of the pelvic floor muscles revealed improvement only in the RF group, with no changes observed in power or perineometer measurements.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors concluded that micro-ablative RF treatment combined with behavioral therapy did not show benefits over sham treatment with behavioral therapy for the treatment of OAB symptoms.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Percutaneous Tibial Nerve Stimulation (PTNS) for the Treatment of Idiopathic Overactive Bladder Jericevic et al (2024) stated that OAB patients who do not achieve satisfactory results with 2nd-line OAB medications should be offered 3rd-line therapies (e.g., BTX-A, PTNS, and SNM).
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: These researchers determined which clinical factors affect progression from 2nd- to 3rd-line OAB therapy.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Between 2014 and 2020, the American Urological Association (AUA) Quality Registry was queried for adult patients with idiopathic OAB.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: For the primary outcome, patient and provider factors associated with increased odds of progression from 2nd- to 3rd-line therapy were assessed.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Secondary outcomes included median time for progression to 3rd-line therapy and 3d-line therapy utilization across subgroups.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: A total of 641,122 patients met inclusion criteria and were included in analysis.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Of these, only 7,487 (1.2 %) received 3rd-line therapy after receiving 2nd-line therapy.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: On multi-variate analysis, patients aged 65 to 79, women, White race, history of dual anti-cholinergic and β3 agonist therapy, metropolitan area, government insurance, as well as single specialty practice had the greatest odds of progressing to 3rd-line therapy.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Black and Asian race, male gender, and rural setting had lower odds of progressing to 3rd-line therapy.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: BTX-A was the most common therapy overall (40 % BTX-A, 32 % SNM, 28 % PTNS).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The median time of progression from 2nd- to 3rd- line therapy was 15.4 months (IQR 5.9 to 32.4).
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Patients less than 50 years old and women progressed fastest to 3rd-line therapy.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: The authors concluded that very few patients received 3rd-line therapies, and the time to progression from 2nd- to 3rd-line therapies was greater than 1 year.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: These researchers stated that the findings of this study high-lighted a potential need to improve 3rd-line therapy implementation.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Furthermore, the American Urological Association/Society of Urodynamics, Female Pelvic Medicine & Urogenital Reconstruction (AUA/SUFU)’s guideline on “Diagnosis and treatment of idiopathic overactive bladder” (Cameron et al, 2024) provided the following recommendation: In patients with OAB who have an inadequate response to, or have experienced intolerable side effects from, pharmacotherapy or behavioral therapy, clinicians should offer SNM PTNS, and/or intra- detrusor BTX injection. (
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Moderate Recommendation; Evidence Level: Grade A) Jefferson et al (2025) stated that procedural therapies for OAB most often include intra-detrusor BTX injections, SNM, and PTNS.
sentences.label: CLAIM

sentences.text: Despite their proven effectiveness, real-world data regarding therapy continuation and cross-over rates between these modalities remain limited.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: In a retrospective, multi-center study, these investigators examined longitudinal patterns of treatment continuation and therapy cross-over among women receiving procedural OAB treatments.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: This trial enrolled women with idiopathic OAB who underwent index BTX, SNM, or PTNS between 2014 and 2022.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Subjects were identified using electronic medical records, and follow-up data were collected through December 31, 2023.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: The primary outcomes included duration of maintenance treatments and cross-over to another procedural therapy for OAB.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: Kaplan- Meier survival analysis estimated rates of continued treatment, and a log-rank test examined differences in cross-over rates among treatment groups.
sentences.label: NON_ARGUMENTATIVE

sentences.text: A total of 506 women met inclusion criteria: 266 (52.6 %) received BTX, 114 (22.5 %) underwent SNM, and 126 (24.9 %) received PTNS.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Ongoing treatment rates were lowest for PTNS, with only 17.1 % maintaining therapy between 3 and 5 years and 26.1 % crossing over to another procedure within 3 years.
sentences.label: PREMISE

sentences.text: Among BTX patients, 65.2 % underwent repeat treatment within 3 years, and 9.5 % of patients crossed-over to another procedure within 3 years.
